era_context: The Roman Empire under Emperor Claudius (41–54 CE) is in the final phase of his reign. Claudius has consolidated the empire and expanded its territories. In Britain, Roman consolidation continues following the invasion of 43 CE. In Judea, Roman procurators govern an increasingly restless population. In China, the Eastern Han dynasty is stable under Emperor Guangwu. In Parthia, the Arsacid dynasty controls the Eastern Mediterranean and Central Asia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most regions lack specific documentation for the year 52 CE; events are assigned by scholarly convention

events.title: Didius Gallus becomes governor of Roman Britain
events.description: Didius Gallus is appointed governor of Roman Britain, succeeding Aulus Plautius (who had led the initial conquest in 43 CE). Gallus's governorship (52–57 CE) is marked by steady consolidation of Roman control rather than dramatic military campaigns. He continues the expansion of the road network and the establishment of forts. His tenure is sometimes criticized by later sources (particularly Tacitus) as lacking aggressive expansion, but modern scholars recognize the importance of consolidation in establishing Roman rule.
key_figures: Didius Gallus (new Roman governor of Britain)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Roman forts and roads

events.certainty_note: The appointment of Didius Gallus as governor of Britain in 52 CE is well-attested in historical sources. His governorship lasted until 57 CE.

events.title: Antonius Felix becomes procurator of Judea
events.description: Antonius Felix is appointed procurator of Judea, succeeding Ventidius Cumanus. Felix's procuratorship (52–60 CE) is marked by increasing unrest and heavy-handed responses to Jewish resistance. He is remembered in Jewish sources as corrupt and brutal. His tenure coincides with the escalation of tensions that will lead to the First Jewish War (66–73 CE). The appointment of Felix marks a turning point in Roman governance of Judea, as his policies will intensify Jewish resentment.
key_figures: Antonius Felix (new Roman procurator of Judea)
events.certainty_note: The appointment of Antonius Felix as procurator of Judea in 52 CE is well-attested in historical sources. His procuratorship lasted until 60 CE.

events.title: Claudius's reign in final phase; court intrigue and succession maneuvering
events.description: Emperor Claudius, now in his 60s, is in declining health. His wife Agrippina (married in 49 CE) wields increasing influence at court. Agrippina is maneuvering to ensure the succession of her son Nero over Claudius's biological son Britannicus. Court factions form around these rival claimants. The year 52 CE is part of the final phase of Claudius's reign, marked by intrigue and the consolidation of Agrippina's power. Claudius will die in October 54 CE.
key_figures: Agrippina the Younger (Claudius's wife, Nero's mother)
events.certainty_note: The general context of court intrigue in Claudius's final years is well-attested. Specific events in 52 CE are not individually highlighted in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the characterization of Antonius Felix as corrupt and brutal is based primarily on Jewish sources (Josephus) written after the Jewish War, which may have exaggerated his faults to explain the causes of the war. Roman sources are less critical of Felix.
historiographic_note: The year 52 CE is sparsely documented. Most sources focus on broader trends rather than individual years. The appointments of Didius Gallus and Antonius Felix are well-attested, but specific events within 52 CE are not highlighted. For Britain and Judea, the significance of these appointments is clear in retrospect, but contemporary sources do not emphasize them. For the Roman court, the general context of intrigue is well-documented, but specific events in 52 CE are not individually recorded. For China, the Eastern Han dynasty is stable, but no specific events in 52 CE are highlighted in surviving sources. The year 52 CE is a transitional year within broader periods of consolidation and escalating tension.

graph_edges.note: Didius Gallus's governorship continued the consolidation phase of the Roman conquest of Britain
Felix's procuratorship contributed to the escalation toward the First Jewish War
